versionNotes: Sprint 4 co-design — students help shape Sprint 4 through 4 aspects with individual reflection + partner discussion
title: Sprint 4 Co-Design
description: Help design Sprint 4. For each of 4 aspects, reflect individually, then discuss with a partner. Switch partners after Aspect 2.

questions.infoBlock:
Aspect 1: What Should We Be Able to Do?

Think beyond specific assignments. After 16 weeks in this course, what should every student be able to do or demonstrate that they could not before? Think about capabilities that would matter to an employer, to your career, to your ability to solve real problems.

Instructions: Write your individual response first. You will share and discuss with a partner next.
questions.prompt: What should every student who completes this course be able to do or demonstrate? Name 2-3 specific capabilities — not topics covered, but things you can actually DO.
questions.placeholder: Example: Every student should be able to (1) take a vague problem from a real stakeholder and turn it into a concrete technical plan without being told what to build, (2) debug and iterate under pressure — like when our Sprint 2 demo broke the morning of and we had to diagnose the API issue live, and (3) explain technical decisions to non-technical people, the way we practiced during stakeholder check-ins.

questions.infoBlock:
Partner Discussion — Aspect 1

Turn to the person next to you. Share your response. Listen to theirs. Then discuss together:

  • Where do your answers overlap? Where do they differ?
  • Think about the entire class — would your answers serve everyone, or just people like you?
  • What capability would you add after hearing your partner?
questions.prompt: After discussing with your partner, write down: (1) your partner's name, (2) the capabilities you both agreed matter most, and (3) anything that changed in your thinking.
questions.placeholder: Example: Partner name: Carlos. We both agreed that being able to scope a project realistically is huge — Carlos pointed out that in Sprint 1, everyone underestimated how long the database setup would take. He added something I hadn't thought about: the ability to give and receive honest feedback without it getting personal. That changed my thinking because I realized I've been avoiding giving critical feedback to my teammates.

questions.infoBlock:
Aspect 2: What Activities Help Us Get There?

Given what you said we should learn in Aspect 1 — what types of activities would actually develop those abilities? Think about what has worked so far in this course and what has not. Consider both in-class sessions and homework.

Instructions: Write your individual response. Same partner discussion follows.
questions.prompt: What types of activities — in class and as homework — would help develop the capabilities you identified? Name at least one activity type that has worked well this semester and one that has not.
questions.placeholder: Example: The stakeholder meetings in Sprint 2 were the most useful activity — having to explain our design to someone who doesn't code forced me to actually understand what I built. The weekly written reflections felt repetitive after a while — I started copy-pasting my own phrasing. What would be better: shorter, more focused prompts that ask one specific question instead of three open-ended ones. For homework, the build logs worked well because they made me document decisions in real time.

questions.infoBlock:
Partner Discussion — Aspect 2

Share your response. Then discuss:

  • Which activities from Sprints 1-3 actually changed how you think or work?
  • Which felt like busywork?
  • If you could design one in-class activity and one homework activity for Sprint 4, what would they be?
questions.prompt: After discussing: (1) your partner's name (same partner), (2) activities you agreed work best, (3) what you would change or add for Sprint 4.
questions.placeholder: Example: Partner name: Carlos. We agreed the Dojo depth sessions were the best — being challenged on our reasoning by the AI made us actually defend our choices instead of just describing them. The design decision analysis felt like busywork because we wrote it after we'd already committed to a direction. For Sprint 4, we'd add a mid-sprint code review where teams swap and critique each other's architecture — like a real pull request review but in person.

questions.infoBlock:
Switch partners. Find a different person to sit with for Aspects 3 and 4.

Aspect 3: How Do We Know We Got There?

Sprint 4 ends with a demo and a portfolio defense. The defense is where you prove your growth across all 4 sprints. How should we assess whether you have actually developed the capabilities from Aspect 1? What counts as evidence? What format lets you show your growth most honestly?

Failing and learning from it IS the learning. A defense that shows you tried something, it broke, and you understood why — that is not lesser evidence of growth. It IS the evidence. The student who shows a polished result they never struggled with has less to defend than the student who shows what went wrong and what they learned from it.
questions.prompt: How should we assess your learning — what counts as real evidence of growth? And what defense format would let you show that growth most effectively? Consider: presentations, portfolio walkthroughs, peer challenges, Q&A sessions, or something else.
questions.placeholder: Example: Real evidence of growth isn't a polished final product — it's being able to explain what I tried that didn't work and why. In Sprint 2, my first prototype completely missed what my stakeholder needed because I assumed I understood their workflow. The evidence is that I can walk through that failure, show how the discovery doc changed my approach, and demonstrate the v2 that actually solved their problem. For the defense format, I think a portfolio walkthrough with live Q&A works best — slides feel performative, but walking through actual artifacts lets the instructor ask 'why did you do this?' and you either know or you don't.

questions.infoBlock:
Partner Discussion — Aspect 3

Share with your NEW partner. Discuss:

  • What kind of evidence would convince a skeptic that you actually grew?
  • Should the defense be 1-on-1 with the instructor, in small groups presenting each other's work, or something else?
  • Think about what would work for the whole class, not just you.

Remember: Evidence of struggle and recovery IS evidence of learning. When discussing what counts as proof of growth, don't dismiss the failures — they are the strongest proof you have.
questions.prompt: After discussing with your NEW partner: (1) new partner's name, (2) what evidence format you agreed on, (3) what defense structure would work best for the class.
questions.placeholder: Example: New partner name: Angel. We agreed that the best evidence format is a 'before and after' comparison — show your Sprint 1 approach to a problem next to your Sprint 3 approach and explain what changed. Angel made a good point that some people are quieter and a presentation format puts them at a disadvantage, so we think a 1-on-1 portfolio walkthrough with the instructor would work best for the whole class. You bring your artifacts, the instructor asks questions, and your answers show whether you actually understand your own growth or are just performing it.

questions.infoBlock:
Aspect 4: What Should Sprint 4 Look Like?

Sprint 4 is weeks 13-16. You have the option to stay with your MDRO stakeholder or choose a new one (with instructor approval). Sprint 4 ends with a demo and a portfolio defense. Given everything you discussed in Aspects 1-3, what should Sprint 4 include to best prepare you?
questions.prompt: What should Sprint 4 look like? Consider: Do you want to stay with MDRO or switch stakeholders? What weekly structure would help? What would make the final 4 weeks feel like they matter?
questions.placeholder: Example: I want to stay with MDRO because we finally understand their workflow and switching would mean starting over. For weekly structure: Week 13 should be finalizing the build with stakeholder feedback, Week 14 should be a dress rehearsal demo with peer feedback, Week 15 prep for portfolio defense, Week 16 the actual defense. What would make this meaningful is if the stakeholder actually uses what we built — even a small part. Right now it feels like a class project. If Piranavan actually deployed our tool, that changes everything about how seriously we take the last 4 weeks.

questions.infoBlock:
Partner Discussion — Aspect 4

Share with same partner as Aspects 3-4. Discuss:

  • If you designed Sprint 4 for the whole class, what would the non-negotiables be?
  • What should be flexible?
  • One concrete recommendation to Prof. Sathya.
questions.prompt: Final discussion capture: (1) partner's name, (2) your group's top recommendation for Sprint 4, (3) one thing that should definitely stay and one thing that should definitely change.
questions.placeholder: Example: Partner name: Angel. Our top recommendation is to have a mandatory stakeholder check-in in Week 13 where we show progress and get honest feedback — not a grade, just 'is this useful?' Keep: the demo format where we present to the whole class, because watching other teams teaches you things about your own project. Change: the reflection prompts should be shorter and more specific — instead of 'reflect on your sprint,' ask 'what's one decision you made this week that you'd make differently now?' That's answerable. The open-ended ones lead to filler.
